content.problemStatement:
You have to type an email to send to your friend. The email can be represented as a string TTT (which does not contain spaces). To complete your task you can perform **any one** of the following actions in one move:


Append a character at the end of the string.
Append a duplicate of the current string.
Find the **minimum** number of moves required to type the email.


Input Format

First-line will contain TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains two lines of input.
The first line of every test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the string.
The second line of every test case contains a string TTT of size NNN.


Output Format
For each testcase, output in a single line - the answer to the iii-th test case.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases <= 5000
S consists only of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the target string, and the second line contains the string S of length N (lowercase English letters).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of moves required to obtain the given string.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: oooobj -> remove 'b' -> oooob -> remove 'b' -> oooo -> halve -> oo -> halve -> o -> remove -> '' (5 moves).\nTest case 2: single character needs one append.\nTest case 3: qqqqr -> remove 'r' -> qqqq -> halve -> qq -> halve -> q -> remove -> '' (4 moves).

solution.approach:
We work backwards from the target string. In one forward move we can either append a single character or duplicate the whole current string (i.e., concatenate it with itself). Reversing these operations gives:
- If the current string length is odd, the last operation could only have been a single‑character append, so we delete the last character.
- If the length is even and the first half equals the second half, the last operation could have been a duplication, so we replace the string by its first half.
- Otherwise the last operation must have been a single‑character append, so we delete the last character.
Each deletion or halving counts as one move. Repeating this until the string becomes empty yields the minimal number of moves, because whenever a duplication is possible we should use it (it reduces the length the most). The algorithm runs in O(N^2) in the worst case (each comparison of halves may scan O(N) characters), which is fine for N ≤ 2000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (worst case when many deletions are performed). With the given limits this easily fits within time limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) additional space besides the input string.
